chunk_summary: This section discusses the importance of supervised WIFS (Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation) workflows in schools, addressing side-effect counseling, adherence strategies, and integration with broader school health programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of supervised workflows in improving WIFS adherence in schools.
Learn strategies to counsel students on managing side effects of WIFS, such as nausea and constipation.
Identify methods to track and address WIFS defaulters and ensure consistent supplementation.
Recognize the importance of linking WIFS with broader school health initiatives like deworming and health screenings.
key_facts: WIFS tablets should be taken after meals to reduce nausea and abdominal discomfort.
Weekly dosing is delivered on a fixed day with supervised consumption in schools.
Constipation is a common side effect; increased fluids and fiber intake are recommended.
Absentees should receive their dose on the next available day rather than waiting a full week.
Adolescents with severe pallor, breathlessness, or syncope require facility evaluation and therapeutic management.
Documentation of the last four doses can serve as an adherence measure during clinic visits.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: “This is weekly medicine to build blood; take it after food; if nausea happens it usually settles, but tell your teacher and do not stop.”
document_key_topics: Integration of WIFS with deworming and health screenings
